article_title: Using a Local LLM as a Zero-Shot Classifier
core_thesis: The article presents a practical pipeline for classifying short, semantically dense free-text data into meaningful categories using a locally hosted large language model (LLM) as a zero-shot classifier, without requiring labeled training data. The author argues that traditional clustering and keyword matching fail on such data due to paraphrase variation and semantic overlap, while LLMs can extract conceptual categories by understanding meaning rather than surface features.
detailed_summary: The author faced a dataset of several thousand short natural-language annotations explaining why automated security findings were irrelevant, which functions to use for a fix, or what coding practices to follow. Traditional methods like embedding similarity (K-means, DBSCAN), topic modeling (LDA), and regex/keyword matching struggled because the text was short, semantically dense, and contained high paraphrase variation. The author then implemented a zero-shot classification pipeline using a local LLM (Gemma 2 9B via Ollama) with three components: candidate categories defined from domain knowledge, a structured classification prompt with low temperature (0.1) and short max output (100 tokens), and a local LLM for inference. The pipeline included preprocessing (removing URLs, boilerplate, normalizing terms, deduplication), classification (running each entry through the LLM with checkpointing every 100 entries), and analysis (aggregating results into a distribution chart). For ~7,000 entries, classification took about 45 minutes on a MacBook Pro. The results showed that over a quarter of entries described non-production environments and another 21.9% described cases where security frameworks already handle the risk, providing actionable insights. The article also discusses when this approach is not suitable (keyword-defined categories, available labeled data, need for sub-second latency, unknown categories) and suggests other applications like customer feedback classification, bug report triage, and code intent classification.

entities.name: Braden Riggs
entities.role: Author of the article and practitioner who implemented the zero-shot classification pipeline.

entities.name: Ollama
entities.role: Tool used to run local LLMs (e.g., Gemma 2, Llama 3.2) for classification without API costs.

entities.name: Gemma 2
entities.role: 9B parameter LLM used as the primary classifier; noted for better judgment on ambiguous entries.

entities.name: Llama 3.2
entities.role: 3B parameter LLM tested as a faster but slightly less precise alternative.

entities.name: Chae and Davidson (2025)
entities.role: Research cited comparing 10 models across zero-shot, few-shot, and fine-tuned regimes, finding large LLMs competitive with fine-tuned BERT on stance detection.

entities.name: Wang et al. (2023)
entities.role: Research cited showing LLMs outperformed state-of-the-art classification methods on three of four benchmark datasets using zero-shot prompting.

key_data_points: Traditional clustering (K-means, DBSCAN) fails on short text because embedding similarity conflates different meanings (e.g., 'development key' vs. 'hardcoded key' produce similar vectors). (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: understanding classification limitations helps in evading detection systems that rely on embedding similarity.)

Topic models like LDA surface words, not concepts, and are too sparse for one-sentence annotations. (🟢 Relevant to programming: when analyzing logs or annotations, topic models may miss semantic categories.)

Regex/keyword matching cannot handle paraphrase variation (e.g., 'test code', 'non-production', 'only used during CI' all mean the same thing). (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: attackers can use paraphrasing to bypass signature-based detection.)

The classification pipeline used a prompt with candidate categories, low temperature (0.1) for consistency, and short max output (100 tokens) for speed. (🟢 Relevant to homelab/devops: this is a concrete configuration for local LLM deployment.)

Preprocessing reduced token budget by ~30% by removing URLs, boilerplate, normalizing terms, and deduplicating by content hash. (🟢 Relevant to programming: practical optimization for any text processing pipeline.)

Classification of ~7,000 entries took ~45 minutes on a MacBook Pro using Gemma 2 (9B). Llama 3.2 (3B) was faster but less precise on edge cases. (🟢 Relevant to homelab: performance benchmarks for local LLM inference.)
The pipeline saved checkpoints every 100 classifications to resume from failures. (🟢 Relevant to devops: robust error handling for long-running batch jobs.)

Over 25% of entries described non-production environments; 21.9% described cases where security frameworks already handle the risk. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: these categories indicate common security exceptions that attackers might exploit.)
methodology_steps: Define candidate categories: Start with a sample of 100 entries, manually classify them, and note recurring themes. Expand to a list of mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive categories (e.g., test code, input validation, framework protections, non-production environments).

Write a classification prompt: Structure the prompt to include the list of themes and the text to classify. Use low temperature (0.1) for deterministic output and limit max tokens (e.g., 100) to get only the label and a brief reason. Example prompt: 'Classify this text into one of these themes: {themes}. Text: "{content}". Respond with ONLY the theme number and name, and a brief reason. Format: THEME_NUMBER. THEME_NAME | Reason'.
Set up local LLM inference: Use Ollama to run a model like Gemma 2 (9B). Call ollama.generate with model='gemma2', prompt=prompt, and options={'temperature': 0.1, 'num_predict': 100}.

Preprocess data: Strip URLs, boilerplate phrases, and formatting artifacts. Normalize common terms (e.g., 'false positive' → 'FP', 'production' → 'prod'). Deduplicate by content hash to remove exact repeats.
Run classification: Iterate over each preprocessed entry, call the LLM, and store the result. Implement checkpointing (e.g., save every 100 classifications) to allow resumption on failure.

Analyze results: Aggregate classifications, count occurrences per category, and generate a distribution chart (e.g., bar chart). Use the distribution to identify dominant themes and inform further analysis.
conclusions: Local LLMs can effectively classify short, semantically dense free-text data into meaningful categories without labeled training data, outperforming traditional clustering and keyword matching on paraphrase variation. The author's pipeline successfully extracted actionable insights from ~7,000 security annotations, revealing that over 25% of entries were about non-production environments and 21.9% about framework protections. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: understanding common security exceptions helps prioritize attack vectors.)

The approach is best suited for medium-scale datasets (hundreds to tens of thousands of entries) with semantically complex text and known candidate categories. It is not appropriate for keyword-defined categories, when labeled data is available, for sub-second latency needs, or when categories are unknown. (🟢 Relevant to homelab/devops: this defines the operational envelope for deploying such a system.)

Temperature setting is critical: low temperature (0.1) ensures nearly deterministic classification, while higher temperatures introduce variability. Limiting max tokens speeds up throughput. (🟢 Relevant to programming: practical hyperparameter tuning for LLM-based classification.)
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your interests in cybersecurity, programming, and homelabbing. For Red Teaming, the technique can be used to classify security annotations, bug reports, or threat intelligence into categories (e.g., false positives, test environments, framework protections), helping you identify common patterns that attackers might exploit. For programming, the pipeline provides a concrete example of using local LLMs for text classification, with code snippets for Ollama integration and preprocessing optimizations. For homelab/devops, the article offers a practical deployment guide for running a local LLM classifier on a MacBook Pro, including performance benchmarks and checkpointing for reliability. As a student, you can apply this method to classify research notes, survey responses, or any unstructured text data, gaining insights without needing labeled datasets.
extra_advice: Consider extending this pipeline to classify security logs or phishing emails in your homelab. You could define categories like 'credential harvesting', 'malware download', 'reconnaissance', and use a local LLM to auto-tag incoming alerts. This would give you a zero-shot threat classification system that runs entirely offline, enhancing your Red Teaming toolkit without API costs.
